TextENGNew: Summer, 2006.
"...There. That should do it."
Having finished sorting through all the handover documents, I stretched my back and arms wide, savoring the small release of tension.
"Quite a mountain of material, as expected.@k@vS05/13/321000003. ...Even so, things have gotten remarkably convenient."
changeReason: Old applies 便利になったものだ to the material ('it was quite useful') but JPN means 'things have gotten remarkably convenient' referring to technology/systems — different referent.

TextENGNew: I glanced at the laptop sitting on the desk in front of me and let out a quiet sigh.
Reports from the field on each and every incident.
changeReason: Old translation specifies 'Crime scene reports,' but the Japanese 現場からの refers more generally to 'reports from the field,' not exclusively crime scenes.

TextENGNew: The various regulations and manuals governing administrative procedures.
And the laws and government ordinances pertaining to public security work...

In the old days, every review meant hauling out documents crammed into thick binders — a thoroughly tedious business. But now, a few keystrokes on this A4-sized device and everything is right there before you.
And with everything stored in a database, the time spent hunting for any given document has been cut dramatically. I'm genuinely grateful for that.
The shelves before me still groaned with the old-era binders, row upon row, but we almost never opened them anymore. They had grown thick with dust.
...And the digitization of records was not the only thing that had changed with the passing years.
One's title, one's surroundings — they shift without pause, sometimes gradually, sometimes with sudden violence.
changeReason: Old translation renders 肩書き as 'job responsibilities' rather than 'title,' and omits 'gradually' (徐々に), losing the contrast between gradual and sudden change.

TextENGNew: Naturally, I could not remain untouched by that current of change...
changeReason: Old translation says he 'struggled to keep up with' change, but the Japanese 無縁ではいられなかった means he 'could not remain untouched by' change--a difference between difficulty adapting and inevitability of being affected.

TextENGNew: "So I'm to be a school principal...@k@vS05/13/321000005. Ha — not exactly the role I would have imagined for myself."
This year, riding the wave of the ongoing trend toward structural reform, the Metropolitan Police Department's public security division underwent a major organizational overhaul.
And come next spring, I was to take up the post of principal at the police academy.
Even so, when I received my transfer notice just a few days ago, I confess I stared at it in blank incomprehension.

Given how the positions narrow toward the top of the pyramid, I had long accepted that a man with no powerful academic faction behind him couldn't stay in public security work forever — but a teaching post was the last thing I had expected.
changeReason: Old translation omits the key detail about lacking a powerful academic faction (学閥), which in the new translation explains why he couldn't stay in public security.

TextENGNew: "I'm sure it's a sign from the gods.@k@vS05/40/321500002. You've been working yourself too hard, Dad, so maybe the universe is telling you to slow down a little."
...Recalling those words my daughter had said with a laugh, I smiled to myself — though with a certain wryness.
Compared to certain colleagues and superiors, I had not worked with any particular ferocity — but that was evidently not how it looked from her side.
Then again, given the nature of public security work, I had never been able to explain to my family what it was I actually did.
changeReason: Old translation claims 'public safety ordinances prevent' disclosure, but the Japanese 公安という特殊な立場上 refers to the inherent nature of public security work, not specific legal ordinances.

TextENGNew: As a result, my daughter had apparently formed some rather exaggerated impression of my work, cobbled together from rumors and media portrayals...
"This view of the 7th Records Room... only a few months left to look at it."
I took another look around me.
changeReason: Old fabricates 'And then I'll never see it again' — JPN 改めて周囲を見やる simply means 'I took another look around me.' Entirely different content.

TextENGNew: ...I had always thought it a bleak and cheerless sight, but now that I was about to leave it behind for good, I felt a faint ache of melancholy.
changeReason: Old says 'when I first took office here' but JPN いざ見納めとなると means 'now that it's time for a last look' — opposite temporal reference (beginning vs end of career).

TextENGNew: This room was, in a sense, a treasury of history — the record of every case my colleagues and I had thrown ourselves against, every battle we had fought, with all their twists and outcomes.
Some cases we had overcome against the odds, exposing the truth and claiming victory.
But others — cases where we had come close, where we had all but grasped the thread of truth — had been forced to a halt, left unresolved, abandoned midway through.
changeReason: Old says 'struggled to find clues' but JPN says they had grasped clues up to the very limit before being forced to stop — opposite implication about investigative progress.

TextENGNew: "...And one of those is... this one."
I worked the keyboard and mouse, passed through the vein-scan security system, and opened the case folder for a particular incident.
changeReason: Old translation says generic 'authentication system' but the Japanese specifies 静脈認証システム (vein-scan authentication), a specific biometric security detail the new translation preserves.

TextENGNew: It was... June of 1983.
It was the documentation on what had come to be called the "Hinamizawa Gas Disaster" — an incident that had occurred in a remote mountain village in the Chubu region.
